text: An emergency resection for laparoscopic heart man procedure video is presented here for a fifty one year old female who came in as an emergency with perforated sigmoid diverticular disease. Patient was positioned in modified Lloyd Davis position with the right tilt. A central umbilical port was used for the camera and the other laparoscopic ports as shown in the diagram.

The initial laparoscopy reveals chronic dilatation, purulent fluid in the pelvis, and a dilated small bowel. The surgeon identifies anatomical landmarks and approaches the inferior mesenteric artery. Monopolar diathermy is used to dissect and expose the artery while preserving surrounding nerves. The inferior mesenteric artery is divided after being secured with hemlock clips.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, dividing the inferior mesenteric vein between clips while carefully avoiding injury to the dilated small bowel. The dissection continues laterally to mobilize the splenic flexure, with a swab placed over the pancreas for protection. The left colon is mobilized using monopolar diathermy.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure by releasing the gastrocolic omentum, revealing the swab placed over the pancreas from the inferior dissection.
The surgeon focuses on the pelvis, where a sigmoid phlegmon is visible. An abscess is observed between the uterus and the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon performs inferior dissection to increase the mobility of the sigmoid phlegmon mass and protect the ureter and gonadal vessels. The left lateral dissection is challenging due to the phlegmon obstructing the surgical instruments, requiring careful navigation to avoid damaging the left pelvic side wall vessels.

The surgeon uses monopolar diathermy to open the abscess cavity, separating the uterus from the diseased sigmoid colon. The abscess is aspirated, and further dissection is performed to assess the condition of the uterus for potential salvage or resection.

The surgeon navigates the challenging area where the left pelvic side wall is adherent, taking care to avoid injury to the left ureter. The fibrotic and hardened tissues are manipulated with precision using energy devices.

The surgeon mobilizes the mass and transects the recto-sigmoid junction at the upper rectum using a 60mm linear stapler. The specimen is extracted through the colostomy site, and an end colostomy is fashioned.

We do share some results of our use of laparoscopy in the emergency setting for benign cases. This approach is technically definitely possible, however a certain degree of experience and team support is needed. The earlier outcomes suggest benefit to these patients in terms of shorter length of stay and improved recovery.
